But if you're discounting $412,000 to $30,000, it doesn't matter what the interest rate is. It's irrelevant, right? So we wanted prepays. We didn't want percentage above base clients. We hated percentage above base clients because we knew what was gonna happen. So we used it as a lever to get them into prepaying. So it was a staged sale. The first part of the sale was, agreed of the percentage above base contract. Well, they all easily agreed of that, right? Because it's nothing. They don't have it. And they think at the moment, hey, if this guy gets me 200 grand or 300 grand over the three years, I'm happy to give him a third of it. I'm delighted. They won't be. But at the moment, they think, who wouldn't love this deal? So they all signed that. That was like 100% close. Then the staged sale was sitting them down setting their goals and making their chart and all that. And then having the realization appear before them of what that 30% was gonna look like. And gee, you could make this all go away now for $36,000. And we got financing for the $36,000. So really, you could make it go away for $817.63 a month. Soup, and we converted them. So that was percentage above base. Royalties are another performance dollars thing. Again, very common, well, not very common, but the thing that top copywriters do, where you pay a fee plus you pay a percentage of gross for as long as you use the copy in order to sell things. Now, sort of the same thing occurs as with percentage above base in some cases. I mean, when I wind up with a client who is still paying me for something, I wrote seven years ago, eight years ago, nine years ago, and every month they're writing a check and I haven't had to touch it since. Some don't mind, they have the right attitude about it. Others are grumpy about it. In some cases, tough. In some contracts, I put buyouts. So they can pay a lump sum fee and make me go away. The per something is then some other way to get paid on performance. Number of people showing up in seminar rooms, reduction in absenteeism, I mean, anything that's measurable, you can tie a performance compensation to as long as you can measure it. The most important thing I will tell you about performance money is it should be fee plus performance money, or at worst, fee has advance against performance money, like a salesman's draw. Not performance money only. Treat the performance money as gravy. Cover your time with the fees, because there's too many ways for you not to get the performance money. It's a copywriter, I got clients, I wrote stuff for four years ago, they've never printed it, never mailed it. And they spent a lot of money for it, but if I was relying on the performance dollars, not good. So the performance dollars to me are like gravy. I get them, I'm thrilled. That goes in a wealth account, but I aid off the fees. Use fees. There, there's licensing fees for work product, for training materials. You get to use my stuff and you pay a license fee in order to use it. There's renewables, you get to use this stuff and then you pay to keep using it, to keep using it, to keep using it. Again, there's per something. Anything that can be measured by use can be charged for by use. So number of sales people who come into this room and watch these DVDs. And how can we control that? Well, the only way to keep modest and count the cookies. The count the cookies thing, by the way, is important. I got that from my theft control expert. Here's the count the cookies example. Takes five minutes to tell it. Mary's at home with little Johnny, little Johnny and Mary, there are no ones in the house. Nobody else is there, no humans, no pets, no animals. Mary bakes 12 cookies. Mary is now gonna walk out to the mail box, get the mail and come back. If she does that without the conversation that I'm about to have, Johnny's gonna eat three, four, five, six, eight, nine, 10, 12 of the cookies while she's gone. So she has to say before she goes, Johnny, I'm going out to the mail box, I'm coming back. Right now, we're gonna count the cookies together. There are 12 of them. When I come back, the first thing I'm gonna do when I walk in the door, before I read the mail, before I do anything else, is we're gonna count the cookies again. And if there ain't 12 cookies here, because you and I are the only ones here and I'm not gonna be here, so if there ain't 12 cookies here, your ass is getting whipped. If she don't have the conversation, the cookies are gone. So anytime you're getting paid based on use, you gotta have a way to count the cookies.
